Fern Polrrrcal PRACTTcEs CovrrrarssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA g5gl4-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
January 9,2007
Morton S. Park, Esq.
Lounsbery Ferguson Altona & peak, LLp
960 Carrterbury Place, Suite 300
Escondido, California 92025
RE: Your Request for fnformal Assistance
Our File No.I-06-227
Dear Mr. Park:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Seth Cohen
regarding the honoraria provisions of the political Reform Act (the ..Act,,) i- gl"u.rr"
your inquiry is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal
asststance.' Please note that this letter should not be construed as advice on any conduct
thatmay have already taken place. In addition, this letter is based o., th. a"t, pr.r"rrt"O.
The Fair Political Practices Commission ("Commission") does not act as a finder of fact
when it renders assistance. (In re ogtesby (1975) 1 Fppc ops. 71; section g31 14.)
QUESTIONS
Do the honoraria provisions of the Act prohibit Seth cohen, a planning
commissioner with the city of San Marcos, from accepting payments for
speaking engagements concerning his areas of knowledgeis a professor at the
University of California San Diego (..USCD,,X
Do the honoraria provisions prevent commissioner cohen from accepting
payments for travel expenses related to the above speaking engagements?
CONCLUSIONS
The honoraria prohibition does not apply to speeches provided customarily, and
in connection with, the practice of a bona fide profession such as teaching.
Therefore, if commissioner cohen's speaking engagements qualify as peisonal
' Go""tnm",,t Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
I 8 1 09- 1 8997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunify provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (section 83114 regulation 1gi29(cx3), copy enclosed). '
1
2
1
EiiGA


services customarilyprovided in connection with his profession as a university
professor, then the honoraria prohibitions would not apply. However, the
"eamed income" exception to the honoraria ban does not apply if the
predominant activity of the profession is making speeches.
2. with regard to payments for traver expenses related to the speaking
engagements, you have not provided any facts or specific details rJgarding the
reimbursements that Commissioner Cohen would ieceive, such as the soulces for
these payments and whether the travel is within Califomia, within the United
States, or outside the country. The rules that apply differ depending on the actual
facts of a case. Therefore, we can only provid; il" with general griidance. see
discussion below.
File No. I-06-227
Page No. 2
Cohen to accept
engagements
Your office represents the City of San Marcos. You received a request for
advice from one of your planning commissioners, Seth Cohen, regarding his receipt of
honoraria for public speaking events.
commissioner cohen is a full-time UCSD professor of chemistry and
biochemistry. He receives numerous invitations to speak on subjects concerning
chemistry and biochemistry. These speaking engagements are unaffiliated with UCSD;
and the organizers of these engagements offer Commissioner Cohen payments for
speaking at the events and related travel oxpenses.
these speech and
concem his areas
Honoraria
FACTS
ANALYSIS
You would like to
travel expense payrnents, provided that the speaking
of knowledge as a UCSD professor.
Section 89502(a) of the Govemment Code provides that "no elected state officer,
elected officer of a local govemment agency, or other individual specified in section
87200 shall accept any honorarium." As a planning commissionei with the City of San
Marcos, Seth cohen is a local official specified in section g7200.
An honorarium is defined as anypayment made in consideration for any speech
given, articlepublished, or attendan ce at atypublic o. p.irui" ;;;f.r;iiJr"rtion,
meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering. (Section 89501(a).) As a public official
specified in section 87200, the honorarium ban applies to Commissioner^Cohen.
However, the term "honorarium" does not include eamed income for personal
services which are customarily provided in connection with the practice of a bona fide


File No. I-06-227
Page No. 3
business, ttade, or profession, unless the sole or predomi nant activity of the business,
trade, or profession is making speeches. (Sections g2030.5 and sesbt@)(1))
'oHonorarium" does not include income earned for personal services ifl
(1) The services are provided in connection with an individual,s business or the
individual's practice of or employment in a bona fide businerr, trud", o.
profession, guch as teaching, practicinglaw, medicine, insuran'"", r"ullstate,
banking, or building contracting, pursuant to California Code of i."gulutiorr,
Title 2, Sections l9932.l through 1g932.3; and
Q)The services are customarilyprovided in connection with the business, trade,
or profession. (Regulation rceZZ.1
An official is presumed to be engaged in the bona fide profession of teaching if
he or she is employed to teach at an accredited university. (Regulation 1gg32.2(a).)
Commissioner Cohen is a full-time professor of chemistry andliochemistry aiuCSD,
which is an accredited _state university. Therefore, he is presumed to be enlaged in the
bona fide profession ofteaching.
In the past, we have advised that presenting lectures and writing articles on
subject matters that are within a professor's area o?expertis" *" g"r"*lly considered
personal services that alre customarily provided in connection witf, the practice of
teaching. (uelman Advice Letter,No. A-99-2g1, copy enclosed.) therefore, if
Commissioner Cohen's speaking engagements concem chemistry and biochemistry, his
areas of expertise as a professor at ucsD, these events could qruiry as personal
services customarily provided in connection with his profession of tlaching.
Nevertheless, the "earned income" exception to the honoraria ban does not apply
if the predominant activity of the profession is making speeches. (Section g9501(bX1).)
Speechmaking is presumed to be the predominant activity of an official,s profession if;
(1) The official has engaged in the profession for one year or more; and


File No. I-06-227
Page No. 4
(2) During the l2-month period before the speech and including the date of the
speech, more than 50 percent of the hours spent on the professi6n are devoted to,
or more than 50 percent of the gross income from the piofession comes from, the
preparation or delivery of speeches. (Regulation lg9i2.3(a).)
we assume that making speeches is not the predominant activity of
commissioner cohen's profession of teaching. However, while not prohibited
honoraria, the Act requires the disclosure of the earned income.
Travel Expenses
The Act and Commission regulations provide exceptions to the honoraria
prohibition and gift limit for certain tlpes of travel payments. (Sections g9501 and
89 506; regulations 1 8950- 1 8950.4.)
The term "ttaYel pa5rment" includes payments, advances, or reimbursements for
travel, including actual transportation and related lodging and subsistence. (Section
8es01(c).)
You have not provided any details regarding reimbursement for travel expenses
that Commissioner Cohen would receive, such as the sources for these payments and
whether the travel is within California, within the United States, or outside the country
The rules that apply differ depending on the specific facts of a case.3 Therefore, we can
only provide you with general guidance in this area. permissible reimbursement of
travel expenses is further explained in the fact sheet,,Limitations and Restrictions on
Gifts, Honoraria, Travel and Loans," and "Travel Guide for califomia officials and
,,
letter
If you have other questions on this matter,please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosures
ERjgl
I:\Advicel.trs\06-227
3 For instance, regulation 18932.4 states that "honorarium" does not include ,,acfrial intrastate
transportation and any necessary lodging and subsistence provided directly in connection with [a] speech,
panel, seminar, or service, including but not limited to meals and beverages on the day of the activity.
These items are not payments and need not be reported by any filer.,, fEmphasis added.]
